children.name: Colius
children.description: Small, soft-plumaged birds with long, graduated tails and a distinctive crest. Mousebirds are named for their habit of scurrying mouse-like through dense vegetation, often hanging upside-down from twigs. They are among the few non-passerine birds capable of clambering acrobatically through branches, using their strong feet and flexible toes.

children.name: Colius striatus
children.description: The most widespread and familiar mousebird, found across much of sub-Saharan Africa from Ethiopia to South Africa. It has a brownish-grey body with fine barring on the neck and breast, a prominent crest, and a very long tail. Highly social, it travels in noisy flocks of up to 20 birds, clambering through gardens and woodland edges.

children.name: Colius colius
children.description: Endemic to southern Africa, found in South Africa, Namibia, Botswana, and Zimbabwe. It is distinguished by a white rump and lower back that contrast with its grey upperparts, as well as a white stripe across the wing. It prefers savanna, scrub, and gardens, often seen in small groups feeding on berries and leaves.

children.name: Colius leucocephalus
children.description: Found in the arid scrub of eastern Africa from Somalia to Tanzania, this small mousebird has a distinctive white crown and nape, a grey body, and a long tail. It inhabits dry thorn scrub and acacia woodlands, often in small groups. It feeds on berries, leaves, and buds, clambering acrobatically through the branches.

children.name: Colius castanotus
children.description: Endemic to Angola, this mousebird has a chestnut-red back, a grey head and breast, and a long tail. It inhabits dry savanna, scrub, and gardens, often in small groups. It is named for its distinctive chestnut-coloured back that distinguishes it from other mousebirds in its range.

children.name: Urocolius
children.description: Slightly more slender than Colius, with longer tails and a red or pinkish bill and face. These mousebirds are also highly social and acrobatic, but tend to inhabit drier, more open savanna and bushveld habitats compared to their forest-dwelling relatives.

children.name: Urocolius indicus
children.description: Widespread across southern and eastern Africa, from South Africa through Tanzania and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It has a grey body, pale blue-green nape, and a distinctive bright red bare patch around the eye. It feeds on fruit, leaves, and buds, often hanging upside-down to reach its food.

children.name: Urocolius macrourus
children.description: Found in East Africa from Sudan and Ethiopia south to Tanzania, inhabiting dry savanna and thorn scrub. It has a grey body with a turquoise-blue nape patch, a red bill, and a very long graduated tail. Like other mousebirds, it is gregarious and acrobatic, often seen scrambling through bushes in search of berries.
